items.subtopic: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport | CYSTINURIA
items.title: Understanding Cystinuria: Genetic Basis and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Cystinuria is an autosomal recessive disorder resulting from defective transporters in the proximal renal tubule and small intestinal cells, leading to impaired reabsorption of specific amino acids. This condition predisposes individuals to kidney stone formation due to cystine's poor solubility.
keyPoints: Worldwide frequency: 1 in 7000
Affects transporters on chromosome 2p16.3 (SLC3A1) and chromosome 19q13 (SLC7A9)
Results in excessive urinary excretion of cystine, lysine, arginine, and ornithine
Cystine stones account for 1–2% of all urinary tract calculi
Stone formation typically manifests in the second or third decade
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport | CYSTINURIA

items.subtopic: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport | CYSTINURIA
items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Cystinuria: Identifying and Quantifying Amino Acid Excretion
items.content: Cystinuria is suspected through the observation of hexagonal crystals in urine or a positive urinary nitroprusside test. Quantitative urine amino acid analysis differentiates between heterozygotes and homozygotes by measuring cystine, lysine, arginine, and ornithine excretion.
keyPoints: Hexagonal crystals observed in acidified, concentrated, chilled urine
Urinary nitroprusside test used for diagnosis
Homozygotes excrete 2400–7200 µmol (600–1800 mg) of cystine daily
Quantitative analysis differentiates heterozygotes from homozygotes
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport | CYSTINURIA

items.subtopic: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport | CYSTINURIA
items.title: Management Strategies for Cystinuria: Preventing Stone Formation and Treating Complications
items.content: The management of cystinuria focuses on preventing cystine crystal formation through increased urinary volume, alkaline urine pH maintenance, and pharmacologic therapy. Penicillamine and tiopronin are used to increase cystine solubility, with surgical interventions as a last resort.
keyPoints: Fluid intake should exceed 4 L/d, ideally 5–7 L/d
Urinary cystine concentration target: <1000 μmol/L (250 mg/L)
Potassium citrate used for urinary alkalinization
Penicillamine and tiopronin increase cystine solubility through sulfhydryl-disulfide exchange
Surgical options include shock wave lithotripsy, ureteroscopy, and percutaneous nephrolithotomy
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport | CYSTINURIA

items.title: Diagnosis and Genetic Basis of Lysinuric Protein Intolerance
items.content: Lysinuric protein intolerance is diagnosed through genetic sequencing, specifically targeting the SLC7A7 gene on chromosome 14q11.2. This gene encodes the y+LAT membrane transporter involved in amino acid transport.
keyPoints: SLC7A7 gene mutation causes lysinuric protein intolerance.
Gene located on chromosome 14q11.2.
Diagnosis confirmed by sequencing SLC7A7, included in hyperammonemia panels.
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · LYSINURIC PROTEIN INTOLERANCE

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Management of Lysinuric Protein Intolerance
items.content: Patients with lysinuric protein intolerance present with hepatosplenomegaly, protein intolerance, episodic ammonia intoxication in childhood, and may develop severe osteoporosis, pancreatitis, impaired renal function, pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, autoimmune disorders, and immune deficiency in adulthood.
keyPoints: Common presentations: hepatosplenomegaly, protein intolerance, episodic ammonia intoxication.
Adult complications: severe osteoporosis, pancreatitis, impaired renal function, pulmonary alveolar proteinosis, autoimmune disorders.
Management includes dietary protein restriction and citrulline supplementation (2–8 g/d).
Nitrogen scavengers like phenylbutyrate, benzoate for persistent hyperammonemia.
Pulmonary disease may respond to glucocorticoids or recombinant human granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor.
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · LYSINURIC PROTEIN INTOLERANCE

items.title: Presentation and Triggers of Citrullinemia Type 2
items.content: Citrullinemia type 2 typically presents with hyperammonemia and neuropsychiatric symptoms between ages 20-50, but can also manifest in neonates as intrahepatic cholestasis. Episodes are often triggered by factors that increase cytoplasmic NADH production, such as acetaminophen, surgery, alcohol, or high sugar intake.
keyPoints: Presentation age: 20-50 years with hyperammonemia and neuropsychiatric symptoms
Neonatal presentation: intrahepatic cholestasis, failure to thrive, dyslipidemia
Triggers: acetaminophen, surgery, alcohol, high sugar intake
Genetic prevalence: ~1:20,000 in individuals of Japanese, Chinese, and Southeast Asian ancestry
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · CITRULLINEMIA TYPE 2 (CITRIN DEFICIENCY)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Citrullinemia Type 2
items.content: Diagnosis is confirmed by identifying pathogenic variants in the SLC25A13 gene. Laboratory findings during an acute attack include elevated ammonia, citrulline, arginine, and galactose-1-phosphate with low or normal glutamine levels. Management includes liver transplantation to prevent disease progression and a ketogenic diet high in fats and proteins, supplemented with medium-chain triglycerides, arginine, and pyruvate.
keyPoints: Diagnosis: pathogenic variants in SLC25A13 gene
Laboratory findings: elevated ammonia, citrulline, arginine; low/normal glutamine; increased galactose-1-phosphate
Management: liver transplantation, ketogenic diet with medium-chain triglycerides, arginine, pyruvate
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · CITRULLINEMIA TYPE 2 (CITRIN DEFICIENCY)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Hartnup Disease
items.content: Hartnup disease is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by pellagra-like skin lesions, neurologic manifestations, and amino aciduria. Diagnosis should be considered in patients with clinical features of pelagra without dietary niacin deficiency history.
keyPoints: Frequency: 1 in 24,000
Defective transporter: B°AT1 encoded by SLC6A19 gene on chromosome 5p15
Symptoms: Pellagra-like skin lesions, neurologic manifestations, amino aciduria
Diagnosis: Neutral amino aciduria detection and genetic testing of SLC6A19 or CTLRN genes
Treatment: High-protein diet and daily nicotinamide supplementation (50–250 mg)
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · HARTNUP DISEASE | CYSTINOSIS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Cystinosis
items.content: Cystinosis is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by cystine accumulation in lysosomes, leading to crystal formation. It presents with renal Fanconi syndrome, photophobia, and other systemic complications.
keyPoints: Gene involved: CTNS encoding the lysosomal cystine/proton transporter (cystinosin)
Clinical forms: Classic nephropathic, juvenile nephropathic, ocular nonnephropathic
Diagnosis: Identification of cystine crystals in cornea and measurement of cystine content in white blood cells or DNA testing of CTNS gene
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · HARTNUP DISEASE | CYSTINOSIS

items.title: Therapeutic Approach to Cystinosis
items.content: Cystinosis management includes cysteamine bitartrate to delay renal failure and address systemic complications. Early initiation of therapy is crucial for efficacy.
keyPoints: Treatment: Extended release cysteamine bitartrate, maintenance dose 1.3 g/m² divided into two daily administrations
Monitoring: White blood cell cystine levels
Additional therapies: Cysteamine eye drops, renal replacement therapy, growth hormone, L-thyroxine, insulin, testosterone
Outcome: With current therapies, life expectancy extends into the late forties
items.reference: Inherited Defects of Membrane Transport · HARTNUP DISEASE | CYSTINOSIS

items.question: What is the maximum solubility of cystine in urine at a physiological pH range?
items.explanation: The maximum solubility of cystine in the physiologic urinary pH range of 4.5–7.0 is ~1200 µmol/L.

items.question: What therapeutic agents are used to form more soluble mixed disulfides with cystine?
items.explanation: Penicillamine and tiopronin undergo sulfhydryl-disulfide exchange with cystine to form more soluble mixed disulfides.

items.assertion: Increasing urinary volume and maintaining alkaline urine pH are part of cystinuria management.
items.reason: This approach helps prevent cystine crystal formation by diluting the urine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Penicillamine is recommended as first-line therapy for all cystinuria patients.
items.reason: It has no significant side effects and effectively prevents stone formation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because penicillamine should be reserved for specific cases due to its potential side effects.

items.assertion: The clinical manifestations of Hartnup disease are due to nutritional deficiencies.
items.reason: Nutritional deficiency of tryptophan and niacin, derived from its metabolism, leads to the symptoms.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Cysteamine bitartrate is used to treat Hartnup disease.
items.reason: Cysteamine bitartrate forms a mixed disulfide with cysteine, which helps in reducing cystine levels.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as cysteamine bitartrate is used for treating cystinosis, not Hartnup disease.
